summary: The post argues that analytics lacks a reliable way to measure itself, leading to circular judgments, bias, and poor incentives. Benn proposes a concrete metric: evaluate analysts by how quickly their work helps others make decisions, because that directly reflects the purpose of analysis and improves communication, focus, and reputation over time.
money_quotes: Analysts should judge their work by how quickly people make decisions with it.
The moment an analyst is asked a question, a timer starts.
That's the only metric we should measure ourselves on--straight up, without qualification or caveat.
This paradox--that analysis can only be measured in circular ways--has real effects that go beyond stressing out armchair philosophers with Substacks.
And that journey starts with a simple step: Ignore all the ambiguity around measuring analytical quality and ROI, and do whatever it takes to make others more decisive.
key_insight: The best way to evaluate analytical work is the speed with which it enables sound decisions.
